# Writing effective documentation

Good documentation is clear, direct, and easy to scan. This guide explains how to approach writing for a technical audience.

## Getting started

In order to write well, you need to understand your audience. Due to the fact that most readers scan rather than read, structure matters more than prose quality in most cases. At this point in time, many teams still write docs as an afterthought rather than treating them as a first-class deliverable.

## Structure and formatting

It is important to note that long paragraphs are difficult to scan. One of the most common things that writers tend to do is bury the most important information at the end of a paragraph.

Make use of headings and bullet points in order to break content into digestible chunks. In the event that you need to explain a multi-step process, numbered lists are preferable due to the fact that they signal sequence.

## Tone

Be direct. Use short sentences. Has the ability to be verbose is not a virtue in technical writing. Instead of saying "you have the option of configuring," say "you can configure."

Avoid phrases that add length without adding meaning. "Gather together your dependencies" can simply be "gather your dependencies." "Close down the application" is just "close the application." "Whether or not" is almost always just "whether."

## Review process

Prior to publishing, ask a colleague to read the draft. In order to get useful feedback, be specific about what you want them to evaluate. At this point in time, most teams review docs informally, which often leads to inconsistencies that are hard to catch after the fact.
